coredata.dc:title: A non-enzymatic photoelectrochemical sensor based on g-C3N4@CNT heterojunction for sensitive detection of antioxidant gallic acid in food
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiaodi
Wang, Chenxi
Chen, Xiaoling
Huang, Minghui
Fu, Qian
Li, Rongjie
Wang, Yilin
Li, Chunyan
Zhao, Pengcheng
Xie, Yixi
Fei, Junjie
coredata.dc:description: Gallic acid (GA) is found in a wide range of natural plants and is relevant to the health of human beings. Here, a photoelectrochemical sensing platform based on g-C3N4@CNT heterojunction has been prepared for the highly sensitive and selective detection of GA. Under the light of xenon lamp, the photocurrent of g-C3N4@CNT is 7 times higher than that of g-C3N4. And the sensor generates 4 times more photocurrent in the presence of GA than without GA. This sensor has a wide linear range from 10 nM to 10 μM with a limit of detection as low as 2 nM. Also, the abundant amino groups of g-C3N4 provide excellent selectivity for the sensor. Furthermore, the sensor can be used for the analysis of GA in black tea samples, which provides a novel and rapid method for the detection of GA in food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical sensor
g-C3N4@CNT heterojunction
Gallic acid
Electron-hole pairs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical performance of g-C3N4@CNT PEC sensing system
Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the g-C3N4@CNT sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the construction of the g-C3N4@CNT PEC sensor and the selective detection of GA.
XRD patterns (A) and FT-IR spectra (B) of CNT, g-C3N4, and g-C3N4@CNT. XPS spectra of g-C3N4@CNT (C) survey spectrum, (D) C 1s spectrum, and (E) N 1s spectrum.
SEM and TEM images of (A, B) g-C3N4, (C, D) CNT, (E, F) g-C3N4@CNT.

(A) DRS spectra of g-C3N4, g-C3N4@CNT heterostructures. (B) Eg estimated by (Ahν)1/2 vs photon energy (hν) plots for g-C3N4, g-C3N4@CNT heterostructures. (C) Photocurrent responses of g-C3N4/GCE (black), g-C3N4/GCE (with GA, red), g-C3N4@CNT/GCE (blue), and g-C3N4@CNT/GCE (with GA, green) in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.0). (D) EIS plot of bare GCE, MWCNTs/GCE, g-C3N4/GCE, g-C3N4@CNT/GCE in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl aqueous solution. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Photocurrent response of the g-C3N4@CNT/GCE to difffferent concentration of GA from 0 (a) to 10 μM (k). (B) The linear relationship between the change of photocurrent and the logarithm value of the GA concentration. (C) Interference of 100 times of proline, leucine, arginine, glucose, galactose, Zn2+, Ni2+, K+ and 50 times of citric acid and 10 times salicylic acid with photocurrent response of the g-C3N4@CNT based on photoelectrochemical platform in the 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7) containing 10 μM GA. (D) specific selectivity of the g-C3N4@CNT sensors. (E) Stability of the GA sensor in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7) without and with addition of 10 μM GA under the Xe lamp.
Detection of GA in real blank tea samples using g-C3N4@CNT PEC sensor.

head.ce:title: A non-enzymatic photoelectrochemical sensor based on g-C3N4@CNT heterojunction for sensitive detection of antioxidant gallic acid in food

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environmentally Friendly Chemistry and Applications of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gallic acid (GA) is found in a wide range of natural plants and is relevant to the health of human beings. Here, a photoelectrochemical sensing platform based on g-C3N4@CNT heterojunction has been prepared for the highly sensitive and selective detection of GA. Under the light of xenon lamp, the photocurrent of g-C3N4@CNT is 7 times higher than that of g-C3N4. And the sensor generates 4 times more photocurrent in the presence of GA than without GA. This sensor has a wide linear range from 10 nM to 10 μM with a limit of detection as low as 2 nM. Also, the abundant amino groups of g-C3N4 provide excellent selectivity for the sensor. Furthermore, the sensor can be used for the analysis of GA in black tea samples, which provides a novel and rapid method for the detection of GA in food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: According to reports, polyphenols are widely present in various natural plants and fruits (Wang, Wang, & Yang, 2007). In recent years, polyphenols have attracted wide interest in all walks of life due to their wide biological significance and potential applications. Gallic acid (GA) is one of the representative components of polyphenols. Its biological properties mainly include anti-inflammatory, antihistamine, anti-tumor activities, scavenging free radicals, and protecting cardiovascular diseases, which are closely related to people's life and health (Abdel-Hamid & Newair, 2011). There is no doubt that it can be seen everywhere in the food and pharmaceutical fields (Diamond, 1998). In the area of medicines, for instance, roses and pomegranates are well-known for their medicinal properties in Uyghur medicine and have been recommended for improving stomach problems and anemia (Wang, et al., 2017). Since natural plants are rich in polyphenols, GA as one of the main ingredients has developed into a reliable indicator for quality control of pharmaceutical companies (Hon, Lau, Hui, & Leung, 2013). In the food industry, GA is a natural antioxidant, and it could be found everywhere in milk tea, fruit juice, edible oil, and red wine. Driven by considerable economic benefits, adulterated products have sprung up in all walks of life, posing a fatal threat to public health. As a result, food safety, closely related to people, has received more attention (Al-Jowder, Kemsley, & Wilson, 2002; Gu, Lan, Shi, & Lu, 2015; Härmä, Peltomaa, & Pihlasalo, 2015). Considering that antioxidants are widely found in various adulterated foods, GA is one of the prominent representatives of antioxidants. The qualitative and quantitative determination of the concentration of GA in food can realize the possibility of food safety monitoring.

In the past few decades, various analytical methods and strategies have witnessed the development and progress of the detection of GA, such as some traditional methods like chromatography, chemiluminescence and electrochemistry (Ma et al., 2013). Unfortunately, most of the established Methods and strategies have inevitable shortcomings. As we all know, chromatographic techniques require complicated and costly instruments, time-consuming operations and skilled manipulators (Liang et al., 2016). Chemiluminescence analysis is easily affected by the external environment and is inevitably limited by poor selectivity (Wang, et al., 2017). As far as electrochemical analysis is concerned, the electrode is prone to fouling during the reaction, resulting in relatively poor stability and reproducibility (Han et al., 2019). In recent years, photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing technology, as an emerging technology, combines the advantages of optics and electrochemistry such as simple detection equipment, low cost, fast analysis speed, and high sensitivity (Zhao, Xu, & Chen, 2015, 2016; Zhu et al., 2018). It has attracted widespread attention, and has been applied to the fields of biology and environmental monitoring (Hao et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2015). The key to PEC sensing technology is the photoelectrode material, because it determines the sensing performance of the PEC sensing platform. Generally, an ideal photosensitive material has a high visible light collection ability, can enhance light absorption and has a fast charge transfer ability to achieve a high carrier separation efficiency and detection signal generation. In addition, to obtain a high-sensitivity detection signal, there are mainly the following two methods: One is to design an appropriate sensitization structure. Previous studies have proved that combining nitrogen and sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) with Zn2SnO4 (Fang, Yang, & Gao, 2018), and combining nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with other photocatalysts (such as MoS2 (Jiang, Du, Zhou, Li, & Wang, 2017), BiOBr (Yin et al., 2016)) can effectively improve PEC performance. The second is to develop new photosensitive materials by constructing heterojunctions. This method is widely used in photoelectrochemical sensing platforms. In addition, choosing the suitable electron donor and electron acceptor is also very helpful in the amplifying the detection signal. Yet, it is important to note that the PEC sensor has a disadvantage of poor selectivity, which limits its application (Bahruji, Bowker, Davies, & Pedrono, 2011; Peng, Li, Zhang, & Gong, 2014), The use of specific materials to modify the electrode can solve the problem of poor selectivity (Heydari, Ghoreishi, & Khoobi, 2019).

g-C3N4 maintains weak van der Waals force between the CN layers, and the layer distance is about 3.3 nm (Motaghed Mazhabi, Ge, Jiang, & Wang, 2018). g-C3N4 has a narrow energy band gap, so it shows high photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation, and shows excellent optical properties (Li, Liu, Cheng, Yang, & Zhang, 2014; Lin et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2013). Compared with other materials, this semiconductor material can collect sunlight, so it has many applications. g-C3N4 as a PEC sensing material has the following advantages, such as long-term stability, simple synthesis and cost-effectiveness, good biocompatibility, excellent film-forming ability, controllable surface and easy functionalization (Amouzadeh Tabrizi, Shamsipur, Saber, Sarkar, & Ebrahimi, 2017; Wang et al., 2015). However, it still faces challenges in practical application, such as the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, and the problem of poor conductivity. Carbon nanotube (CNT), as a typical carbon nanomaterial, has excellent conductivity and high specific surface area. The combination of g-C3N4 with the CNT, CNT can act as an electron acceptor and electron mediator, thereby providing enhanced photo-induced charge transfer capability, facilitating the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, improving photoelectric conversion efficiency, and improving photocatalytic activity.

Based on this, we successfully constructed a photoelectrochemical sensor based on the g-C3N4@CNT heterojunction for the sensitive and selective detection of GA. The corresponding interface carrier transfer mechanism is studied. Under light excitation, photoexcited electrons transition from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of g-C3N4, while holes (h+) are generated in VB and CNT serves as the role of electron mediator and electron acceptor, GA acts as a hole acceptor, thereby increasing the separation rate of photogenerated electrons and holes, and generating higher photocurrent (Fig. 1 ). Under the open-circuit voltage, the change of photocurrent has a linear relationship with the logarithm of the concentration of GA. The linear range is 10 nM–10 μM, and the detection limit is 2 nM. The sensor platform has good stability and repeatability. This is the first photoelectrochemical sensor based on g-C3N4@CNT composite material to detect GA. Just important is that the sensor has been used for the GA analysis in black tea samples and is expected to be used in the analysis and determination of antioxidants in foods, laying a foundation for the determination of antioxidants in foods by unlabeled PEC.

MWCNT was purchased from Jiangsu Xianfeng Nano Material Technology Co., Ltd., with a diameter of 5–15 nm, an inner diameter of 2–5 nm, a length of 10–30 μm, and a purity of >95%. Melamine, ethyl gallate (EG), propyl gallate (PG), catechin (CC), baicalin (Bn), rutin (Ru), ascorbic acid (AA), arbutin (Ab), p-phenylene Diphenol (HQ), resorcinol (Rs), salicylic Acid (SA), citric Acid (CA), l-leucine, l-arginine, l-proline, glucose, galactose, Ni(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. NaCl and KCl were purchased from Hunan Huihong Co., Ltd., and NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. All chemicals were used without further purification. Phosphate buffer was made of sodium phosphate (NaH2PO4 / Na2HPO4, the molar ratio is 1:1) and sodium chloride, and they were dissolved in deionized water to a final concentration of 0.1 mol L-1 (pH = 7). GCE electrodes were sonicated with absolute ethanol and ultrapure water for 5 min after polishing with Al2O3 powder, respectively, and dried under an infrared lamp. Black tea was purchased from the supermarket.

All electrochemical experiments were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI650E, CH Instruments, Chenhua Co., Shanghai, China). Materials are synthesized in a high temperature tube furnace (MFLGKD206, Shanghai Muffle Furnace Technology Instrument Co., Ltd). The working electrode was a modified glassy carbon electrode, and the counter electrode and reference electrode were platinum wire and Ag/AgCl electrode, respectively. AC impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz, measured in a mixed solution of 5 mmol L-1 [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 mol L-1 potassium chloride aqueous solution Income. A Xe lamp (PLS-FX 300 HU, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.) was used as the light source. The morphology and structure of the material were characterized by scanning electron microscope system (SEM, JEOL, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100*), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 DISCOVER) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250Xi). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS, UV-2550*) and steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy (PL, Hitachi U-4600) were used to characterize the light absorption capacity of the material and the separation ability of electron-hole pairs.

The g-C3N4@CNT heterojunction was synthesized according to the one-step calcination method reported in the reference (Song et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 1, melamine (500 mg) and MWCNT (5 mg) were completely ground in a quartz mortar. In a nitrogen atmosphere, the resulting mixture was calcined in a tube furnace at 550 °C for 4 h (5 °C/min) to produce a lump of blue-gray solid called g-C3N4@CNT, which was ground and stored. For comparison, in the absence of CNT, pure g-C3N4 was synthesized in the same way. Take the above two materials and make a 2 mg/mL solution for use.

Before the measurement, the GCE electrode was ultrasonically cleaned with absolute ethanol and ultrapure water in turn, and then dried under an infrared lamp and set aside. When preparing the working electrode, use a pipette to drip 6 μL of 2 mg/mL g-C3N4@CNT solution onto the surface of the dried GCE electrode and dry it under an infrared lamp. After that, the modified electrode was immersed in a PBS solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.0), and the three-electrode system was irradiated with a Xe lamp as a light source. The photocurrent response was recorded at a voltage of 0 V.

According to the previous literature (Abdel-Hamid & Newair, 2013), the black tea samples were prepared with slight modifications. Weigh 100 mg of dry black tea into a 100 mL round-bottomed flask, add 40 mL of ultrapure water, and stir continuously on an oil bath for 15 min at 90 °C. On this basis, the tea liquid was filtered. The filtrate was cooled to room temperature, sealed and stored in the refrigerator for measurement. When testing, take 50 μL of the above-prepared filtrate in 50 mL PBS solution, which was equivalent to 1000 times dilution, and the photocurrent response was recorded to detect the content of GA in black tea.

The XRD patterns of CNT, g-C3N4 and g-C3N4@CNT composite materials are shown in Fig. 2
A. CNT has two very obvious diffraction peaks at 26.3° and 43.4°, which are attributed to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of the hexagonal graphite material, respectively(Song, et al., 2020). The peak of pure g-C3N4 at 13.0° is attributed to the (100) crystal plane of the triazine ring structure, and the peak at 27.5° is attributed to the (002) crystal plane of the graphite-like interlayer stack structure (Wang et al., 2022). The XRD pattern of g-C3N4@CNT is similar to that of pure g-C3N4, and it seems that there is no characteristic peak of CNT, which may be attributed to their low content and high dispersion in the sample (Ding, Zhao, Yang, Zhao, Chen, & Jiang, 2019).

In Fig. 2 B, FT-IR spectroscopy further confirmed the effective binding between g-C3N4 and CNT. It can be seen that CNT has no obvious absorption peak due to its symmetrical structure. For pure g-C3N4, there is a strong absorption peak at 1200–1700 cm−1, attributed to the characteristic aromatic skeleton vibration of the CN heterocycle (Li, Chen, Wang, Sun, & Antonietti, 2011). And the absorption peak at 810 cm−1 belongs to the typical respiratory vibration absorption peak of the triazine ring (Xiang, Yu, & Jaroniec, 2011). In addition, there is a broad absorption peak at 3000–3450 cm−1, which is caused by the NH stretching vibration of the amino functional group on g-C3N4 (Tong, Yang, Sun, & Jiang, 2015). The spectra of pure g-C3N4 and g-C3N4@CNT are very similar, and the peak position does not change much. On the one hand, the content of CNT is relatively small. On the other hand, it shows that the introduction of CNT does not destroy the structural integrity of g-C3N4 (Ding et al., 2019). However, it can be seen from the figure that the g-C3N4@CNT nanocomposite has a stronger FTIR mode, and the peak intensity is obviously enhanced, especially the NH stretching vibration absorption peak becomes more obvious. This shows that adding CNT is beneficial to increase the exposure of g-C3N4 surface functional groups (Ding et al., 2019), especially to increase the exposure of functional groups amino groups. As we all know, the electrode process of GA is controlled by adsorption (Luo, Li, Li, & Luo, 2013; Tashkhourian & Nami-Ana, 2015). The exposure of amino functional groups will be more conducive to the adsorption of GA on the electrode surface, which can make the sensor have better detection performance for GA. At the same time, each vibration absorption peak on the g-C3N4@CNT spectrum of the composite material becomes stronger, which further proves the successful synthesis of the composite material from the side.

To study the chemical structure of g-C3N4@CNT and further demonstrate the successful recombination of the material, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is shown in Fig. 2 C, D, E. The XPS survey spectra of g-C3N4@CNT (Fig. 2 C) shows that the material contains C, N, and O three elements, peak signals are located at 288, 399 and 532 eV, respectively. The O1s signal is attributed to surface-adsorbed oxygen-containing hydroxyl species (Liu, Zhang, Wang, Dawson, & Chen, 2011). The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 D) showed three peak signals after peak fitting: at 284.8, 286.4 and 288.2 eV, respectively. These peaks are attributed to adventitious hydrocarbons and CC bonds in CNT (Xiang, Yu, & Jaroniec, 2011), C-NHx and CN-C in g-C3N4, respectively (Yu et al., 2017). The high-resolution N1s curve (Fig. 2 E) shows three peaks with binding energies of 398.6, 399.9and 401.2 eV, respectively. The peak at 398.6 eV is due to sp2-N (CN-C) in the triazine ring, this illustrates the existence of sp2-bonded graphite-like g-C3N4. The peak at 399.9 corresponds to the N atom bound to three C atoms (N-(C)3) and the peak formed at 401.2 eV is attributed to the NHx group (Ge, Han, Liu, & Li, 2011). Which can also confirm the successful recombination of g-C3N4 and CNT.

The performance of a sensor is closely related to the morphology of the modified material (Ghoreishi, Khoobi, Behpour, & Masoum, 2014). The surface morphology image was detected by SEM and TEM. Pure g-C3N4 is composed of many irregular sheets clustered together, the whole presents a block shape (Fig. 3
A, B). The CNT are fibrous in the SEM image (Fig. 3 C), and the tubular shape can be clearly seen in the TEM image (Fig. 3 D). After the introduction of CNT, the g-C3N4 aggregates were dispersed to a certain extent, and the sheet-like g-C3N4 and tubular CNT were cross-distributed, and the uniform growth was combined into one (Fig. 3 E, F). This proves not only the synthesis of the composite but also the good contact between the two materials.

Through UV–vis DRS spectrum analysis to characterize the light absorption capacity of each material (Fig. 4
A). It can be seen that the light absorption edge of g-C3N4 is located at 454.2 nm, and the light absorption edge of g-C3N4@CNT is located at 542.7 nm. It is obvious that the light absorption edge of g-C3N4@CNT is obviously red-shifted, with a significantly wider light absorption range, and the absorption capacity in the visible region is significantly enhanced. This may be due to the π-π conjugation between CNT and g-C3N4, which makes it possible to obtain higher light absorption capacity. Based on the Tauc plot method, a graph of (Ahv)1/2 vs hv was made (Fig. 4 B), and the band gaps of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4@CNT were estimated to be 2.7 eV and 2.3 eV, respectively. The narrowing of the band gap of the composite material further confirms that the introduction of carbon nanotubes can improve the light absorption performance.

The modified electrodes were fabricated with g-C3N4 and g-C3N4@CNT to obtain the photocurrent curve characterizing the behavior of PEC. Fig. 4 C shows the comparison of the photocurrent response of each modified electrode. It is found that the electrode modified with single-material g-C3N4 only generates 3nA photocurrent, which may be due to the influence of morphology. The morphology and texture of g-C3N4 photocatalyst are crucial to improve the photocatalytic performance (Wang et al., 2019). g-C3N4 is agglomerated together by flakes, which makes the separation rate of electron-hole pairs not high, resulting in low photocurrent. For the electrode modified with g-C3N4@CNT, a higher photocurrent of 20 nA is obtained. This shows that CNT can act as a PEC sensitizer for g-C3N4, forming a heterogeneous structure to play a synergistic effect, so that the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs is reduced. At the same time, the incorporation of CNT makes the agglomerates of g-C3N4 dispersed and the morphology is improved, thereby obtaining a higher photocurrent.

In addition, the charge carrier mobility of each material is characterized by the EIS diagram (Fig. 4 D). The arc radius in the Nyquist curve can reflect the resistance of the interface layer generated on the electrode surface. A smaller arc radius indicates a smaller electrochemical charge transfer resistance (Rct) and a higher charge transfer efficiency (Bai et al., 2013). The composite material g-C3N4@CNT Nyquist semicircle radius is significantly smaller than the single material g-C3N4, which indicates that the doping of CNT makes the resistivity of electron transfer is reduced, so the conductivity is higher. The above shows that the doping of CNT enhances light absorption and conductivity, thereby generating the stronger photocurrent.

To obtain the ideal PEC sensor performance, the pH value of the electrolyte and the influence of the operating voltage on the response current are evaluated in Fig. S2
(A, B). The pH range increases from 4.0 to 7.0, and the photocurrent increases. This may be due to the fact that peracid will consume the amino group on g-C3N4, which will affect the adhesion of the detection substance GA on the electrode surface, resulting the change of photocurrent is not apparent in the presence of GA. When the pH continues to increase until it is alkaline, the change in photocurrent decreases sharply with the increase in pH. This could be due to the target GA is an acidic substance, it will decompose under alkaline conditions, so it has little detection effect on GA. Ultimately, choose PBS buffer (pH = 7) as an electrolyte.

The working potential is closely related to the PEC performance, and has a great influence on the photocurrent. A relatively negative applied potential makes the cathode photocurrent higher, but it will also cause a larger measurement deviation due to electronic drift (Zhang et al., 2015). It can be seen from Fig. S2
(C, D) that the change of the photocurrent of g-C3N4@CNT reaches the best at 0 V, so in the following experiment a voltage of 0 V was finally used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of g-C3N4@CNT PEC sensing system
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the best experimental conditions, the real-time photocurrent response of the composite material g-C3N4@CNT modified electrode under different concentrations of GA was tested, to explore the analytical performance of g-C3N4@CNT as a photoactive substance. As shown in Fig. 5
A, the intensity of photocurrent increases as the concentration of GA increases, and the change value of photocurrent has a linear relationship with the logarithm of the concentration of GA. The linear range is 10 nM − 10 μM, there is a turning point at 100 nM. The two linear regression equations are listed below (Fig. 5 B).
Δ I = 8.06
l g C - 5.18
(
R
2
= 0.9940 ,
C
G A
:
10
n M - 100
n M )
Δ I = 19.81
l g C - 29.73
(
R
2
= 0.9948 ,
C
G A
:
100
n M - 10
μ M )

Following the analysis function of LOD = Kσ/S, the LOD of the sensor is calculated to be 2 nm. (defined as K = 3) (MacDougall et al., 1980). Compared with some previous reports (Table S1), our work shows a very wide linear range and a low detection limit. Apparently, this is attribute to the introduction of CNT, which makes the amino groups on the surface of g-C3N4 more exposed, which can more effectively bind to the abundant carboxyl groups on the detection substance GA, which makes this sensor perform better.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the g-C3N4@CNT sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-interference ability of g-C3N4@CNT sensor was explored by adding interfering substances coexisting with 10 μM GA in the electrolyte. Among them, proline, leucine, arginine, glucose, galactose, Zn2+, Ni2+, K+ are the added GA is 100 times, citric acid is 50 times, and salicylic acid is 10 times. The photocurrent response after adding interfering substances is almost the same as the photocurrent generated by only containing GA (Fig. 5 C), which shows that the g-C3N4@CNT sensor has excellent anti-interference performance. The specific selectivity capability of the g-C3N4@CNT sensor was explored by adding a structural analog coexisting with 10 μM GA in the electrolyte. These structural analogs include some common antioxidants and some other polyphenols, and the amount added is 2 times that of GA. As can be seen from Fig. 5 D, the sensing platform exhibits good selectivity. The good selectivity of this sensing platform is presumably due to the abundant amino groups on g-C3N4@CNT, at the same time, GA contains many hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Which makes it possible to preferentially detect gallic acid rather than other interfering substances. In addition, differences in redox potential can also lead to differential detection of the target species in the valence band position (Kilmartin, Zou, & Waterhouse, 2001; Rodrigues, Silva Ferreira, Guedes de Pinho, Bento, & Geraldo, 2007).

Good stability and reproducibility are the keys to the sensor (Ghoreishi, Behpour, Mortazavi, & Khoobi, 2016). The main factors affecting stability include photocorrosion and photocatalyst poisoning during the detection process (Wang, et al., 2017). In the absence and presence of GA, the sensor has been continuously monitored for a long time to evaluate the stability. It can be seen when the light is turned on, the photocurrent rises instantly but quickly stabilizes. After 20 on/off cycles, the photocurrent remains almost unchanged (Fig. 5 E). To study the storage stability of the g-C3N4@CNT modified electrode, the g-C3N4@CNT modified electrode was stored in the refrigerator and taken out after 30 days. The photocurrent was still 89% of the original. All this shows that the designed sensor exhibits satisfactory stability. Six electrodes were prepared under the same conditions to test the reproducibility of the sensor. As shown in Fig. S3 , the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the sensor was calculated to be 3.7%, which shows that the prepared PEC sensor has good reproducibility.

The feasibility test of the g-C3N4@CNT sensor system in black tea was carried out through standard recovery experiments, and the results are shown in Table 1 . The prepared black tea was diluted with PBS buffer to obtain a black tea sample, and the content of GA in the black tea sample was successfully detected. Then, different concentrations of GA standard solution were added to the black tea sample. Through the determination, the recovery rates were 99.5%, 99.2% and 100.1%, respectively. RSD is in the range of 0.9–3.5%. Which indicates the feasibility of the sensor in actual sample detection.

It is well known that photogenerated holes preferentially react with species with lower redox potentials to cause an increase in photocurrent (Han et al., 2019). The redox potential of GA is about 0.24 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), while the redox potential of PG, EG, Bn, Ru, HQ, glucose is higher than that of GA, which makes g-C3N4@CNT sensor will preferentially respond to the GA. From the molecular electrostatic potential map of GA ( Fig. S4 ), O1 and O2 will be preferentially oxidized. However, some interferents have little difference with the redox potential of GA, or lower than it. It can be seen from Fig. 5 C, D, these interferents also have little interference on the detection of GA, this indicates that the interaction of the amino groups on g-C3N4@CNT with the carboxyl groups on GA makes the GA concentration higher near the working electrode. Under the stimulation of light, the electron-hole pairs in g-C3N4@CNT are separated to a certain extent, resulting in the generation of photocurrent. In the presence of GA, holes are consumed, the separation rate of photogenerated carriers is improved, and higher photocurrent is obtained, thereby realizing qualitative and quantitative detection of GA (Fig. 1). This provides an idea for designing sensors based on the interaction between materials and objects.

In this work, we fabricated a non-enzymatic sensor for the detection of antioxidant GA based on g-C3N4@CNT. CNT has three main functions in this work. One is to act as an electron mediator and electron acceptor, and GA acts as a hole acceptor, thereby reducing the recombination rate of charge carriers; the other is to increase the conductivity of the composite material, accelerate electron transfer, and obtain a higher photocurrent signal; The third is to increase the exposure of the functional group on g-C3N4, especially the exposure of amino groups, contributed to the selectivity of the sensor. The g-C3N4@CNT sensor has a wide linear range (10 nM − 10 μM), low detection limit (2 nM, S/N = 3), high sensitivity, good stability and excellent anti-interference ability. This is the first photoelectrochemical sensor based on g-C3N4@CNT composite to detect GA. Moreover, the sensor has been used for the GA analysis in black tea samples. It is expected to be used for the analysis and determination of antioxidants in foods, laying a foundation for the determination of antioxidants in foods by unlabeled PEC.

Jiaodi Li: Conceptualization, Methodology. Chenxi Wang: Writing – original draft. Xiaoling Chen: Validation. Minghui Huang: Visualization. Qian Fu: Investigation. Rongjie Li: Visualization. Yilin Wang: Methodology. Chunyan Li: . Pengcheng Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yixi Xie: Supervision, Validation. Junjie Fei: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874114, 21775133, and 22176160), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2021JJ30662), the Project of Innovation Team of the Ministry of Education (IRT_17R90), the Project of Science and Technology Innovation Plan of Hunan Province (2021WK2006), the Project of Science and Technology Plan of Hunan Province (2017XK2055), and the Open Research Fund of Key Laboratory of Polar Materials and Devices, Ministry of Education.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.133086.

ce:section.ce:para:
